title: Wokwi IoT Device Simulation with AWS IoT Core Real-Time Dashboard

tasks.title: Task 1: Sign in to AWS Management Console

steps.text: Click on the Open Console button, and you will get redirected to AWS Console in a new browser tab.

On the AWS sign-in page, Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12 digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab. Now copy your User Name and Password in the lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12 digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab.
Now copy your User Name and Password in the lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Once Signed In to the AWS Management Console, Make the default AWS Region as US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1.
Make sure you are in the US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1 Region.
Navigate to IoT Core by clicking on the Services menu in the top, then click on IoT Core.
From the left menu on Bedrock page click on Policies Under Security
On the Policy page click on Create policy button.
Task 2: Create an IoT Policy
For policy name: Enter whiz-policy
Task 2: Create an IoT Policy
For Policy Document Select JSON remove the existing and copy paste the below policy
Task 2: Create an IoT Policy
Click on Create button
Task 2: Create an IoT Policy
On the left side menu Click on Thing under All devices.
Click on Create things button.
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing
For Number of things to create: Select Create single thing and click on Next button.
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing
For Thing name: Enter whiz-thing
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing
Leave rest as default and click on Next button.
For Device Certifications leave it as default and click on Next button.
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing
For Attach Policies select whiz-policy created above and click on Create thing button.
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing
Download all the 5 certificates and keys in your local and click on Done.
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing
Once certificates get downloaded navigate to downloads folder to view downloaded files.
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing
Now first we will rename the files we will be using them later to configure authentication for the device
Rename the certificate.pem.crt file to : device.pem.crt
Rename the private.pem.key file to : private.pem.key
Task 3: Create an IoT Thing

tasks.title: Task 4: Activate the Wokwi license key in your browser

steps.text: Download the "AWS_IoT_Core" file and extract it.
Navigate and Open the "aws_iot_core" folder.
Task 4: Activate the Wokwi license key in your browser
Open the "diagram.json" file click "Get your license key" then click "Open".
Task 4: Activate the Wokwi license key in your browser
Click "Get your license" log in with your email or Gmail ID then click "Get your license" again.
Task 4: Activate the Wokwi license key in your browser
Click Get your license.
Once the popup appears in your browser, click "Open Visual Studio Code" allow the Wokwi Simulator extension check the checkbox and click "Open".
Task 4: Activate the Wokwi license key in your browser
?7. If you get the following notification, click Do not ask me again, and Select Open.

tasks.title: Task 5: Attach the AWS IoT certificates in the code

steps.text: Navigate to AWS iot core service, then Click “Connect one device”
Scroll down to find and copy the endpoint command, e.g.: arti9nb0f3kjog-ats.iot.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
Task 5: Attach the AWS IoT certificates in the code
Open VS Code aws_iot_core src secrets.h
Replace the existing endpoint "aws_end_point" in the secrets.h file
Task 5: Attach the AWS IoT certificates in the code
Copy and paste the certificates in “secrets.h” : Amazon Root CA1, Device Certificate, and Private Key in the respective place.
Task 5: Attach the AWS IoT certificates in the code
Now, Copy paste the “device_certificate” and “private.key”
Open main.cpp, Click the tick button on the top right to compile
Task 5: Attach the AWS IoT certificates in the code

tasks.title: Task 6: Create a public S3 bucket

steps.text: In this task, we are going to create a new S3 bucket in the US East (N. Virginia) region with a unique name disabling ACLs, and allowing public access for hosting the static website.
Navigate to S3 by clicking on the Services menu at the top, then click on S3 in the Storage section.
In the S3 dashboard, click on the Create Bucket button.
In the General Configuration,
Select Bucket Type : General purpose
Bucket name: Enter “lab-iot-dashboard”
AWS Region: Select US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1
Task 6: Create a public S3 bucket
Object ownership: Select ACLs disabled (recommended) option
In the option of Block Public Access settings for this bucket, Uncheck the option of Block all public access.
Check the I acknowledge that the current settings might result in this bucket and the objects within becoming public checkbox.
Task 6: Create a public S3 bucket
Keep everything default and click on Create Bucket button.
Task 6: Create a public S3 bucket

tasks.title: Task 7: Upload the HTML file and Enable Static Website Hosting settings

steps.text: In this task, we will enable static website hosting for our S3 bucket, configure it to use index.html and error.html, copy the provided endpoint, upload two files, and configure the bucket policy by copying its ARN and pasting the provided policy code.
To proceed, go to the S3 bucket name that you created and click on it. After that, navigate to the Properties tab which can be found at the top of the screen.
Scroll down to the Static website hosting section and click on Edit button.
Task 7: Upload the HTML file and Enable Static Website Hosting settings
In the Static website hosting dialog box
Static website hosting: Select Enable
Hosting type: Choose Host a static website
Index document: Type index.html
Error document: Type error.html
Click on Save Changes.
Task 7: Upload the HTML file and Enable Static Website Hosting settings
Go to the Properties tab of your S3 bucket, and find the Static website hosting section. Copy the Endpoint provided in this section to your clipboard and save it for future reference.
The next step is to download the zip file by clicking on the replace the le html_file, extract it, and upload two files named index.html and error.html to the S3 bucket you created earlier.
Task 7: Upload the HTML file and Enable Static Website Hosting settings
In the Permissions tab, Click on the Edit button beside the Bucket Policy.
Task 7: Upload the HTML file and Enable Static Website Hosting settings
You will be able to see a Blank policy editor.
Before creating the policy, you will need to copy the ARN (Amazon Resource Name) of your bucket.
Copy the ARN of your bucket to the clipboard. It is displayed at the top of the policy editor. it will look like ARN:“arn:aws:s3:::your-bucket-name".
In the policy below, Update the bucket ARN on the Resource key value and paste the below policy code in the editor.
Task 7: Upload the HTML file and Enable Static Website Hosting settings
Click on Save changes button.
In this task, we are going to create a Lambda function which is used to process the device messages.
Navigate to the services menu at the top, then click on Lambda under the Compute section.
Click on the Create a function button.
Choose: Author from scratch.
Function name: Enter “WhizFunction”
Runtime: Select Python 3.13
Architecture : x86_64
Click “Change default execution role” -> choose “Use an existing role”
Select “Lambda_role_ xxx”
Task 8: Create a Lambda Function
Leave other fields as default and click on Create function button
Task 8: Create a Lambda Function
If you scroll down a little bit, you can see the Code source section.
Remove the existing code in AWS lambda lambda_function.py Copy the below code and paste it into your lambda lambda_function.py file.
Task 8: Create a Lambda Function
Replace the bucket name.
Task 8: Create a Lambda Function
Save the function by clicking on Deploy button.

tasks.title: Task 9: Create an IoT Core rule

steps.text: In this task, we will create an AWS IoT rule which will call Lambda function to process the messages from the device to AWS IoT core.
Open the IoT Core console on the left side menu click on Rules under Message routing.
Click on Create rule button.
Task 9: Create an IoT Core rule
For Rule name: Enter whiz_rule and click on Next button.
Task 9: Create an IoT Core rule
For the SQL Query Statement copy paste the below sql statement and click on Next button.
Task 9: Create an IoT Core rule
Now to Attach rule actions search for Lambda and select Lambda action.
For Lambda function: Select WhizFunction
Lambda function version: Select $Latest
Task 9: Create an IoT Core rule
Review all the details and click on Create button.
Task 9: Create an IoT Core rule

tasks.title: Task 10: Check IoT messages in the AWS IoT Core test client

steps.text: In this task, we will test message sending and receiving using the MQTT client in the AWS IoT Core dashboard.
On the AWS IoT Core console, click on MQTT test client and Click on the Subscribe to a topic tab.
Task 10: Check IoT messages in the AWS IoT Core test client
For topic filter : Enter iot/lab and click on Subscribe button.
Scroll down to view the subscription.
Task 10: Check IoT messages in the AWS IoT Core test client
Now, click on Publish to a topic tab.
Task 10: Check IoT messages in the AWS IoT Core test client
For Topic name: Select iot/lab
For the the Message payload copy paste the below message and click on Publish button.
Task 10: Check IoT messages in the AWS IoT Core test client
To verify the message, go to the “Subscribe to a topic” tab in the MQTT client dashboard.
Task 10: Check IoT messages in the AWS IoT Core test client
You will see the incoming messages displayed in real time as they are published.

tasks.title: Task 11: Run the wokwi code and check AWS IoT Core test client

steps.text: Open the aws_iot_core folder, then navigate to the src directory and open the main.cpp file. Wait 2–3 minutes
To compile the code, click the checkmark button in the top right corner.
Task 11: Run the wokwi code and check AWS IoT Core test client
Once the code compiles successfully, open the diagram.json file.
Task 11: Run the wokwi code and check AWS IoT Core test client
Click the “Run” button. Please wait 2–3 minutes, as it may take some time to connect to AWS IoT Core.
Task 11: Run the wokwi code and check AWS IoT Core test client
Once successfully connected, the device will start sending data in JSON format.
Task 11: Run the wokwi code and check AWS IoT Core test client
To verify the data is being sent, go to the AWS IoT Core MQTT test client.
In the “Subscribe to a topic” tab, enter the topic used in your code (e.g., iot/lab) and click Subscribe.
You will see the JSON-formatted messages appear in real time — this confirms successful data transmission from your device to AWS IoT Core.
Task 11: Run the wokwi code and check AWS IoT Core test client

tasks.title: Task 12: Check the live website and ensure data updates in real time

steps.text: Go to the S3 bucket where your files are stored. Click on the index.html file to open its details.
Copy and click the “Object URL” from the file overview section — this is the public link to access the web page (if permissions allow).
Task 12: Check the live website and ensure data updates in real time
The index.html file will open as a webpage. You should now see your hosted web content rendered in the browser.
Task 12: Check the live website and ensure data updates in real time
Click to change the value from the sensor . (vscode)
Task 12: Check the live website and ensure data updates in real time
The updated data will be sent through AWS IoT Core and you will see the values update live on the webpage.
Task 12: Check the live website and ensure data updates in real time
If the data does not update on the webpage, try rerunning the Wokwi simulator. After restarting, wait for it to reconnect to AWS IoT Core, and the live data should begin updating again.
Task 12: Check the live website and ensure data updates in real time
Once the lab steps are completed, please click on the Validation button on the left side panel.
This will validate the resources in the AWS account and shows you whether you have completed this lab successfully or not.
https://labresources.lab.com/d4af9350a40053467c4d40549623c9a7/chrome-capture-2025-8-5_37_26.gif
You have successfully created an IoT Policy
You have successfully created an IoT Thing
You have successfully created a Wokwi simulation dashboard using VS Code.
You have successfully created a Lambda Function.
You have successfully created an IoT Rule.
You have successfully created and launched an Amazon S3 bucket.
You have successfully configured the bucket to host a static website.
You have tested your static website to make sure it works correctly.
Sign out of AWS Account.
You have successfully completed the lab.
Once you completed the steps, click on End Lab from your lab lab console and wait till the process gets completed.
